title: 【Apple新CEOは“技術”に原点回帰】スティーブ・ジョブズの「プロダクト主義」が復活／しかし“iPhone級の革命”はもうない／Bloomberg記者マーク・ガーマン【WORLD DECODER】

transcript.text: iPhone.
iPhone.
Okay, we have Mark German, Bloomberg
Zapper reporter on the show. Thanks for
coming on the show, Mark.
>> Thanks for having me. Good to be here.
>> Yeah,
>> exciting times.
>> I wanted to ask you about this first
when we spoke in January and you said I
remember you said that there was no
chance that Tim Cook was stepping down
this year and uh we were over a year
away from that happening. And you you
also said the good earnings is a clear
driver of why Tim Cook is not out right
now. So, did the leadership change uh
happen earlier than you had expected or
why do you think this happened now?
>> Uh, I think it turns out that the
blockbuster holiday quarter earnings was
actually uh the reason that Tim Cook got
to write his own ticket. It was a good
way to buoy uh the situation for John
Turnis to take over at a very opportune
time. Uh so, I think it did both things.
I think it helped protect Tim Cook. Uh
but it also helped this become an
opportune time for that leadership
handoff to actually take place.
>> Mhm. What was your reaction when you uh
found out about the leadership change?
>> Uh I think it happened exactly as I
anticipated. Uh my strong belief for 2
years now uh is that John Turnis who is
the senior VP of hardware engineering
will be the next CEO. given the
circumstances surrounding AI right now,
they needed someone a bit younger in the
seat, someone who has more depth for the
modern technologies uh occurring in the
world right now and that made turn the
the ideal fit.
>> Mhm. So, how did Apple uh employees, the
people inside Apple react to uh this
announcement and and whatever suppliers
and stakeholders of Apple? I think
there's a lot of enthusiasm uh within
Apple for this new CEO. They see this as
a new direction, a new product and
engineering focused direction. Uh
putting engineering and science back at
the center of the company versus the
operations side of the equation. Uh from
a supplier perspective, I think they're
happy that this is happening as well
because now there's going to be all
sorts of cool new products uh for them
to be hired by Apple from. So there's
going to be a lot more money-making
opportunity for those suppliers in Asia.
>> Great. Um so um tell us about you know
about John Turners. What kind of leader
is he from your standpoint?
>> You know John Turnis is a leader that uh
elevates the people below him. He's a
leader that is very in tune and in-depth
on the details. He's very in touch with
the product side of the organization. uh
he's very politically savvy. Uh he has
respect for those who came before him
and I think overall he's going to do a
great job as the as the CEO of Apple.
>> Mhm. What was the major achievement uh
that he has done uh during his tenure at
Apple?
>> The major achievements uh there's
there's a few. One he helped bring the
original iPad to market in 2010. He was
running the product design function for
for that machine. He was also pretty
influential and instrumental in the move
for the Mac from Intel to in-house chips
and we've known that has done wonders
for the Mac business. Uh he helped build
the 5G iPhone 12 5 years ago and that
was pretty monumental for that
smartphone line. Uh but overall during
his tenure as senior VP of hardware
engineering the big takeaway is that
he's upped the performance upped the
battery life upped the reliability and
upped the longevity uh of the product
line and so I think that is his legacy
from the hardware engineering
perspective.
>> So in terms of the being the CEO um how
is he different uh from Tim Cook? You
know, Tim Cook picked his spots around
dealing with governments, uh, dealing
with the finance side of the business,
the sales side of the business, the
operations side, maybe the retail side
of the business. That's not John Turnis'
area of expertise. Instead, Turnis is
going to be very focused on engineering
and the product side and using the
resources of the company to build around
uh, products, helping them in AI, uh,
helping them in design, helping them in
software. So, I think uh that Turnis is
going to be a very very product focused
CEO. Uh no one's ever going to be, you
know, exactly similar to to Steve Jobs,
but I think it's fair to say uh that in
terms of the product focus, we're going
to see more of that.
>> And you just wrote uh his big profile uh
a while ago, and you mentioned that he
has a reputation of being a nice guy.
How does uh those reputation uh impact
his leadership?
>> Uh well, I think empowering those below
him being a collaborator
uh not taking no for an answer and
really pushing in maybe a more gentle
way uh the the company to work on the
products that are are necessary that he
believes they should be brought to
market. Bringing the rest of the
organization in tune with each other,
breaking down silos. I think that's
going to be a big part of Turnis'
leadership.
>> So, it's a pretty good thing uh for him
to run the company.
>> I think it's a good thing for the
company for sure. Yeah, I definitely
think it's a good thing for Turnist to
be in charge. Putting product back at
the center of of the organization I
think is something that they've needed
for a while. uh you know as we've seen
the last you know several years Apple
has fallen behind in some key new areas
whether it's wearables whether it's AI
uh whether it's other types of hardware
like headworn devices AR VR and I think
turnis being so in tune with the modern
technologies of the industry is really
going to elevate their product
development processes and the types of
products they're able to bring to market
and then the speed they're able to bring
them to market. Mhm. So you you reported
and talked about uh how Turners had been
getting more and more responsibilities
over time uh in the company and so why
did Tim Cook and the board ultimately
pick him uh for the next CEO? uh they
picked John Turnis because they needed
to give someone a singular mandate of
bringing more types of new products to
market and expanding and upping the
overall device portfolio at the company
and integrating it all deeply with AI.
And he's the one who has this hardware
software integration experience to bring
new devices to market. Uh and so I think
they thought he would be the most
effective CEO from a product standpoint,
from an innovation standpoint, from a
vision standpoint, someone who could be
more of a singular decision maker. Uh
the other component here that is pretty
important uh is the fact that he's 50
years old which gives him a 15 to 20
year runway in the job. Uh Apple's board
didn't want to appoint someone for the
short term. They wanted to appoint
someone who could be running the company
over a decade and a half. Just like in
the same way that Tim Cook was able to
run Apple uh for 15 years himself as
CEO. Just like Steve Jobs was able to be
in the CEO seat also for about a decade
and a half. Obviously, he passed away
and that was premature. Uh, but Tim but
Steve Jobs had been in the in the seat
for over a decade as well. Apple doesn't
want to do a 2, threeear CEO like you
saw with Disney recently and have to go
through the succession game again in a
few years. Uh, they wanted it to be cut
and dry.
>> So, of course, his capability is an
important factor, but his age was also
an important factor. Uh yeah, like I
said, the age is a very important
factor. Being 50 years old, that gives
him a 15-year runway, maybe a 20 year,
even 25 year runway uh in the C as CEO.
And it's very similar to what we saw
with Tim Cook who was able to be CEO for
15 years from 50 to 65. We saw similar
with Steve Jobs who was in the CEO seat
for nearly 15 years as well. Obviously,
that ended prematurely uh due to health
reasons, but uh he still was in that
chair for for nearly 15 years, too. Mhm.
I want to d uh dip into the product
product specific topics later. But when
you look at the high level management at
Apple, what is he expected to do as new
incoming CEO and what kind of challenges
will he be uh facing in the road ahead?
>> Yeah, I mean the first challenges that
Turnis is going to need to confront uh
are the AI challenge and helping Apple
fix their AI situation. he's going to
need to confront the challenge that the
company needs innovative new types of
products. Uh and perhaps the biggest
issue he's going to face is being able
to retain the company's top talent.
There are a lot of people here who in
this transition are getting leveled.
They're going down a b uh going down a
role, right, in terms of title and
position. And he's going to need to do
everything he can to fight and retain
the right people. Mhm. Do you think he's
going to struggle with the mass recent
mask exodus of longtime executives by
Apple?
>> Well, I think that Apple right now, you
have a lot of executives at the top of
the company who've been there for nearly
four decades, 40 years, believe it or
not. And so, you're going to see him
need to remake much of that executive
team uh in the coming few years or so.
And so, that's going to be something
fascinating to watch.
>> Mhm. So, is he going to uh build a
leadership team from the ground up uh
when you when he took the role?
>> Well, I think there's a few people uh
who've come in recently and they wanted
to get those new roles in before Turnis
starts and obviously uh he's well aware
of who those people are. Probably helped
interview him in some interview them in
some such. Uh but definitely I think the
whole executive team is going to get
remade other than the new COO uh and the
new CFO and the new general counsel.
There's a lot of slots there uh that
you're going to see new people in
probably in the coming years. You think
of things like services. You think of
things like software. You think of
things uh like the um some of the the
the corporate services side of the
company, right? Those are roles that are
likely going to get refreshed the next
few years.
>> Mhm. And uh about him, you also wrote
that he laughed off the idea Apple
should worry about being late to
generative AI. So um but AI is of course
important uh the management issue uh at
Apple. So what do you what do you think
is going to deal with the AC issue?
>> Yeah, I'm you know excited to see what
he does about AI. He's launching a new
AI platform in the hardware engineering
group to bring the company up to speed,
let it develop products faster with more
reliability. I'm curious how much work
he's going to be doing with Craig
Federigi on the software engineering
side to sort of speed things up on the
AI front there, too. Are they going to
bring new AI people in there? That's an
open question. So, I certainly think
there's a lot to to to figure out on the
AI front. And for his part, you know,
he's been pushing AI pretty heavily, uh,
at least from a hardware standpoint,
launching all sorts of new wearables in
the in the not too distant future.
>> Okay, moving on to the second part. What
will happen to iPhone ultimately? So I
mean of course not just iPhone the suite
of products uh Apple has but the
Japanese audience is very curious about
the where the iPhone is going and how
John Turners is going to change it. So
what do you think iPhone is going from
your perspective uh in this new era of
Apple?
>> Yeah. Well the iPhone is going to go
foldable this fall uh in September. I
think it's going to be a pretty
significant launch. This is going to be
the biggest overhaul in the history of
the iPhone since it launched in 2007.
And I think people are going to clamor
for this device and this technology. And
you know, I think it's going to be
pretty innovative uh like you've seen
from foldable phones from other uh
manufacturers. Uh the other thing to
watch is uh the changes uh to the the
the iPhone development cycle. You're
going to see a uh a two-prong approach.
You're going to see launches now in the
spring and fall. also going more
aggressive in splitting the line between
the high-end and the low end and doing
them at different times of the year
which I think is going to you know
extend revenue growth uh across more
parts of each calendar. Uh then the
other thing you're going to see is this
20-y year anniversary phone at the end
of 2027 which is also going to be quite
significant and very high performant. So
I think the iPhone is going to continue
to have a long life and legacy uh under
Turnis but he's going to have to work
overtime to to meet the needs of
consumers 5 10 years from now. uh with
something that could ultimately replace
the iPhone.
>> Yeah. You've been talking about uh this
road map of iPhone, but how will the
leadership of Jun Turners uh change the
course of iPhone or contribute to uh the
future development of iPhone?
>> Uh I don't think much is going to
change. He was already owning the the
iPhone hardware roadmap. I think what
will change is maybe he'll put some more
pressure on the software and AI
organizations within Apple to sort of up
the software and up the AI components of
these devices. And maybe he's going to
work overtime on what eventually will
replace the iPhone uh or augment the
iPhone to a larger degree than what we
see today. But in terms of the phone
itself, that's not going anywhere.
>> Mhm. Do you think he's going to take the
stage at WWDC uh uh in the coming June
and the announce the revamped Siri and
big announcement?
>> I don't know. Uh I would be curious to
see who is going to take the stage at
WWDC. I think Craig Federigi who does
software engineering is going to own a
lot of that. I think Tim Cook is
definitely going to be in the WWDC
keynote. You know, he's still going to
be the CEO at that time. Uh so I totally
imagine that Tim Cook is going to be
front and center for that and I think
Tim Cook uh definitely wants to finish
off the AI story with the Siri before he
uh hits the road.
>> Mhm. Uh about turning the company into
AI centric, what do you think about um
the John Turner's thinking uh about like
a combination of AI and hardware right
now? uh what do you think he's going to
do
to sort of the uh turn the company flip
the company upside down and like it
transform the company?
>> Yeah. You know, they're working on
several new AI smart home devices,
several new AI wearables, and releasing
new hardware that's built around
artificial intelligence and Siri and
Apple intelligence is a way to bring
this technology to market in sort of a
unique cohesive way. uh and so launching
all these products in in short order I
think is going to be a boon for the
corporation.
>> So um when we talk about the product
also the supply chain is really
important and which Tim Cook is good at.
So how does this change of leadership
impact those operations side and how is
John Tunis uh going to be working on
that?
>> Yeah, it's a good question. you know I
think he's going to leave the
operational side of the company to the
operations experts uh people like Sabi
Khan people like Priya Basil Braian uh
people in the sales organization and the
finance organizations Kevin Perk Mike
Fanger uh I think that side of the
business is largely going to going to
keep humming uh and turn is going to put
a lot of his you know uh attention and
time on the hardware engineering the AI
the software side of the company
>> what was the Tim Cook's legacy uh in the
first
Tim Cook's legacy was being able to have
Apple carry on uh after the the death of
Steve Jobs. It was no certainty that
Apple was going to be able to continue
to the degree he was able to continue
and he kept that chip moving uh kept
that ship moving very fast. Uh the other
thing he was able to do was expand the
product portfolio and expand the reach
uh of Apple. He did a deal with China
Mobile to ship the iPhone to billions of
new customers in China. They did the
larger iPhone, they did the iPad Pro,
they did many types of AirPods, many
types of iPhones, many types of Apple
Pencils. So, uh, he was able to grow
Apple in terms of how they sell, where
they sell, um, the countries they're in,
uh, how many stores they have, the
different channels they're in, uh, but
also in variations of many types of
products, and the cherry on top, a
couple new product categories as well,
AirPods, the Apple Watch, and obviously
the Apple Vision Pro, which hasn't been
successful, but maybe be the beginning
of something bigger. Mhm. Yeah. I was
also wondering that what the um unsolved
issue of Apple that Tim Cook didn't get
to solve and then John Turners is going
to handle with during his tenure.
>> Yeah. The well a couple things. One is
AI. He's going to need to confront and
fix AI. Uh the next thing he's going to
be able he's going to need to bring new
product categories to market. Uh and
then the third thing that they're
probably going to have to deal with is
how to relocate the silicon supply chain
uh from Taiwan to elsewhere in the
world. You know, 99% of Apple chips
through TSMC are still built in Taiwan.
Uh that is a huge risk factor and
they're going to have to diversify and
do a better job of that to do it much
more quickly.
>> Mhm. Okay. Before we wrap up, um um my
final question is do you think John
Turners can ship some kind of once in a
generation kind of products like a
transformational product uh during his
future tenure?
>> I don't see anything akin to an iPod uh
or an iPhone being shipped during the
the turn 10 year. I mean maybe smart
glasses, right? if they are that much
better than what you're seeing from
Meta, potentially the AR glasses, but I
would say there's going to be a lot of
iPadsized hits, which is which is pretty
good. But, you know, we both know
there's never going to be another
iPhone.
>> Mhm. Okay, great. Um, so Mark, thanks
for being the show. Uh, it's always
good. Great to have you here.
>> Pleasure. Thank you very much.
>> Thank you so much. See you next time.
>> Yeah. See you.
